1:
“Take heed that ye do not your alms before men, to be seen of them: otherwise ye have no reward of your Father which is in heaven.” The witnesses note that Christ here turns from outward acts to the inward motive that makes or mars them. The grammatical-historical commentators discuss the reading: Lightfoot and Gill argue the original word is “righteousness” (the better text), of which almsgiving is one branch, so that the warning covers all religious duty, not alms only. Robertson notes the verb “take heed” (prosechete) calls for vigilance. Wesley, for the Wesleyan, sums the chapter’s theme: having described “inward holiness,” Christ now teaches “that purity of intention without which none of our outward actions are holy.” Calvin, for the Reformed, says Christ exhorts his people “to do what is right before God, and not to make a parade before men.”

The fathers warn against the subtle danger of vainglory. The Eastern Chrysostom observes that “the desire of fame is near akin to virtue,” a beast “hard to be discerned” that “steals upon” the doer of good; Pseudo-Chrysostom: “when anything truly glorious is done, there ostentation has its readiest occasion.” The Latin Augustine confesses how strong is the love of human glory — “none feels it but he who has proclaimed war against it” — and Gregory warns that “if herein we seek our own glory,” our deeds “are already cast out of His sight.” The Gloss reads the verse as Christ fulfilling the Law “in respect of promises,” that we may do God’s commandments “for heavenly wages, not for the earthly.” The witnesses thus find in the verse the governing principle of true religion: that the worth of a good deed lies in its motive. To do righteousness “to be seen of men” is to seek and to get a human reward, and so to forfeit the reward of the Father; but to do it before God, for his glory, is to lay up treasure in heaven. The witnesses note that Christ does not forbid being seen — for our light must shine (Matthew 5:16) — but forbids the doing in order to be seen; the eye must be on God, not on men.
2:
“Therefore when thou doest thine alms, do not sound a trumpet before thee, as the hypocrites do in the synagogues and in the streets, that they may have glory of men. Verily I say unto you, They have their reward.” The witnesses apply the principle to almsgiving and expose the hypocrite. The grammatical-historical commentators explain “hypocrite” (hypokritēs) as “taken from stage-players, who act the part of others” (Barnes), so the ostentatious giver merely acts a part. On “sound a trumpet,” they divide: Robertson and Lightfoot doubt a literal trumpet (no such Jewish custom is documented), reading it as a vivid metaphor for making a public show, though some take it of the trumpet-shaped alms-chests or of literally summoning the poor (Wesley: “under a pretence of calling the poor together”). The Gloss marks the place (“streets and synagogues”) and the motive (“that they may receive honour of men”).

The fathers press the solemn “they have their reward.” The Latin Jerome and Augustine identify the trumpet-sounder as the hypocrite who courts applause; Gregory carefully distinguishes from hypocrites those who, doing good openly, do not seek their own glory. The Eastern Pseudo-Chrysostom sets alms, prayer, and fasting as “three chief virtues” opposed to the three temptations Christ overcame. Calvin, for the Reformed, notes that Christ “reproves a long-established custom” of giving where men would see. The witnesses thus find in the verse a sharp warning and a closed account. The hypocrite gives to be seen, and is seen; he seeks the glory of men, and gets it — “they have their reward” in full, and may look for nothing from God. The witnesses note the irony of the phrase: the applause of men is the whole and only wage of ostentatious charity, paid in the coin the giver chose. To trade the reward of the Father for the praise of the crowd is a poor bargain; and the disciple is warned that the moment he gives in order to be admired, he has already received all he will ever receive.

The witnesses note the searching honesty the verse demands. It is possible to give generously and yet to give for self, so that the very charity that helps the poor robs the giver of his reward. The verse thus turns the disciple’s attention from the amount of his gift to the aim of his heart, warning that ostentation can spoil the best of deeds. To give for the praise of men is to be paid in praise, and to be left with nothing before God; the wise giver therefore seeks no audience but heaven.
3:
“But when thou doest alms, let not thy left hand know what thy right hand doeth.” The witnesses take this as a proverbial command of extreme secrecy. The grammatical-historical commentators agree it is “a proverbial expression, signifying that the action should be done as secretly as possible” (Barnes), in “free hyperbole, extremest secrecy” (Ellicott) — so private that, if it were possible, the giver would scarcely know it himself (Gill). Clarke notes that “works of charity must” often “be hidden from even our nearest relatives.” Wesley qualifies it wisely: “do it as secretly as is consistent with the doing it at all,” and with “the most disinterested intention.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, draws the heart of it: “we ought to be satisfied with having God for our only witness, and to be so earnestly desirous to obey him that we shall not be carried away by any vanity.” The witnesses thus find in the verse a vivid call to single-minded charity. The figure of the right hand giving without the left hand’s knowledge presses the disciple to such secrecy of motive that he gives not for the eyes of others, nor even to admire his own generosity, but purely for God and the need of the poor. The witnesses note that the command is aimed less at literal concealment — for some charity must be open — than at the death of self-regard: the giver is to forget his own gift, banishing the inward applause that would rob it of its worth. To give and not to dwell on the giving, to do good and let it slip from one’s own mind, is the spirit Christ commends; the alms that the left hand never knows of are the alms that the Father in secret will surely reward.

The witnesses add that this hidden charity is the mark of a heart set on God rather than self. The right hand that gives freely and the left hand kept in ignorance picture a generosity so pure that it leaves no room for vanity. The verse thus calls the disciple to do good and forget it, to bless others and not to dwell on the blessing he confers. Such is the charity that pleases God — open-handed, single-eyed, and content that he alone should know.
4:
“That thine alms may be in secret: and thy Father which seeth in secret himself shall reward thee openly.” The witnesses dwell on the secret giving and the seeing Father. The grammatical-historical commentators note that Robertson and others judge the word “openly” to be no part of the genuine text — “Jesus does not promise a reward for private piety” as a public display, but the reward of the Father who “seeth in secret.” Gill recalls the Jewish “secret chamber” where alms were brought privately for the poor. Clarke presses the comfort and the warning: “the eye of the Lord is upon us,” seeing “not only the act, but every motive that led to it.” Ellicott reads it as “a principle rather than a rule” — publicity may be a duty in public work, but private charity should be hidden.

Calvin, for the Reformed, meets the objection that Scripture also bids us edify by good example (Matthew 5:16): the two are reconciled by Christ’s design, which forbids not the being seen but the doing in order to be seen. The witnesses thus find in the verse the great motive that frees charity from vanity — the seeing Father. The disciple gives in secret not because his deed is worthless, but because it is seen by One whose notice is enough: the Father “which seeth in secret.” The witnesses note the sufficiency of this audience: the giver needs no human witness, for God beholds both the gift and the heart, and his reward is sure. To know that the Father sees is at once the cure of ostentation and the comfort of obscurity: the humblest secret kindness, unknown to men, is fully known to God, and what is done for his eye alone he himself will reward. The witnesses thus read the verse as turning the disciple’s gaze from the crowd to the Father, content that He sees, and assured that He repays.

The witnesses dwell on the comfort of the seeing Father for all who serve unseen. Much of the best work of the kingdom is done in obscurity, unknown and unthanked by men; the verse assures such servants that nothing is hidden from God, who beholds both the deed and the heart and will himself repay. To labour for his eye alone is to labour with the surest of rewarders, and to be freed at once from the craving for human notice and the fear of human neglect.
5:
“And when thou prayest, thou shalt not be as the hypocrites are: for they love to pray standing in the synagogues and in the corners of the streets, that they may be seen of men. Verily I say unto you, They have their reward.” The witnesses note that the same vice that corrupted almsgiving corrupts prayer. The grammatical-historical commentators explain that the hypocrites “manifested the same spirit about prayer as alms-giving: it was done in public places” (Barnes); Lightfoot and Ellicott describe the Jewish custom of praying standing at the appointed hours, which the ostentatious contrived to be doing “in the synagogues and corners of the streets,” where crowds would see them (Robertson). The Eastern Chrysostom calls them hypocrites because, “feigning that they are praying to God, they are looking round to men.”

The fathers guard the point: it is not the public place but the public motive that condemns. Augustine: Christ “does not now bid us pray, but instructs us how” — “not that the mere being seen of men is impiety, but the doing this in order to be seen.” Cyprian bids us “pray secretly in remote and withdrawn places,” assured that “God, who is present everywhere, hears and sees all.” Calvin, for the Reformed, calls it “a gross and shameful profanation of the name of God” when hypocrites “pray in public to obtain glory from men.” The witnesses thus find in the verse the exposure of prayer turned into performance. To pray in order to be seen is to address men under the form of addressing God — a hollow show that gets the reward it seeks, the admiration of onlookers, and nothing more. The witnesses note the special gravity of the sin in prayer: in almsgiving the vainglorious at least help the poor, but the man who prays to be seen turns the most sacred act, the soul’s converse with God, into a stage for himself. Such prayer, the witnesses observe, never reaches heaven, for it was never truly directed there; “they have their reward,” and it is all of earth.

The witnesses press the peculiar danger of a religion of display in the very act of prayer. To turn the soul’s converse with God into a performance for men is to empty prayer of its essence, addressing the crowd under the guise of addressing heaven. The verse thus warns that the holiest exercise may become the hollowest, and bids the disciple guard the secret of his heart even in his devotions, that he may pray to God and not to the gallery.
6:
“But thou, when thou prayest, enter into thy closet, and when thou hast shut thy door, pray to thy Father which is in secret; and thy Father which seeth in secret shall reward thee openly.” The witnesses set the secret closet against the public show of the hypocrite. The grammatical-historical commentators explain the “closet” (tameion, an inner chamber or storeroom, Robertson) with the shut door as the image of withdrawn, private prayer, free from the eyes of men. The reward, again, is the Father’s, “which seeth in secret” (the word “openly” being doubtful in the text, as in v.4). The Latin Cyprian had urged this very thing — to “pray secretly in remote and withdrawn places, as best suited to faith.”

The witnesses are careful that the closet is not a new law against common prayer. They note that Christ himself and the apostles prayed in public and with others (cf. Acts 1:14); the command strikes at the motive, not the place. The point, the fathers agree, is that prayer must be directed to God alone, and the surest guard against praying to be seen is to pray where none can see. The witnesses thus find in the verse the positive counterpart to the warning. Against the prayer that courts an audience, Christ sets the prayer of the shut door — the soul alone with God, unobserved and unconcerned to be observed, pouring out its petitions to the Father “which is in secret.” The witnesses dwell on the intimacy and the assurance of it: the closet door shuts out the world and shuts the disciple in with God, who sees in secret and hears in secret and will reward. Such hidden prayer, the witnesses note, is the truest, for it has no eye but God’s and no aim but to be heard by him; and it is to this secret communion, not to the parade of the streets, that the Father gives his reward.

The witnesses find in the shut door an emblem of the intimacy God invites. To withdraw from every eye and pour out the heart to the Father who sees in secret is the privilege of the child of God, who needs no witness but his Father. The verse thus commends the discipline of private prayer, where the soul, alone with God, may speak freely and be heard surely. The closet is not a prison but a meeting-place, and the door that shuts out the world shuts the believer in with the God who hears.
7:
“But when ye pray, use not vain repetitions, as the heathen do: for they think that they shall be heard for their much speaking.” The witnesses note the second fault in prayer — empty wordiness. The grammatical-historical commentators dwell on the word “vain repetitions” (battalogēsēte): Barnes, Clarke, and Robertson trace it to “stammerers who repeat words,” or to a poet Battus of endless verses, denoting “mere babbling or chattering, empty repetition.” Lightfoot illustrates from the heathen’s long-winded supplications. The fault, Calvin and Wesley note, is not length itself but “words without meaning,” the “superfluous and affected repetition” by which the heathen hoped to wear down their gods.

The fathers diagnose the unbelief behind such prayer. The Latin Augustine notes that “the Gentiles think they shall be heard for their much speaking,” and the Gloss adds that “a multitude of words were needful for the Gentiles, seeing the demons could not know” their wants without being told. Gregory contrasts true prayer, which “consists rather in the bitter groans of repentance than in the repetition of set forms.” The Eastern Chrysostom warns also against “asking improper things,” as “dominions, fame, overcoming of enemies.” Jerome guards against the opposite error of the Epicureans, who from God’s foreknowledge concluded prayer needless. The witnesses thus find in the verse a warning against the heathen view of prayer. To suppose that God is moved by the mere quantity of words, or worn into answering by endless repetition, is to think of him as the heathen thought of their idols — ignorant, reluctant, to be informed and importuned. Christian prayer is not the battering of a deaf god with many words, but the trustful approach of a child to a Father who already knows; and the disciple is to pray, not at length to be heard, but in faith and meaning, knowing that few words from a sincere heart prevail more than the heathen’s endless babble.

The witnesses observe that the verse does not condemn earnest or repeated prayer — for Christ himself prayed thrice in the same words in Gethsemane — but the mechanical heaping of words without heart. The fault is to trust in the quantity rather than the sincerity of prayer, treating God as the heathen treated their idols. So the verse calls the disciple to pray with meaning and faith, knowing that the Father is moved not by the length of our words but by the truth of our hearts.
8:
“Be not ye therefore like unto them: for your Father knoweth what things ye have need of, before ye ask him.” The witnesses ground the rejection of vain repetition in the Father’s perfect knowledge. The grammatical-historical commentators stress that prayer “is not designed to inform God” (Clarke; cf. Luke 12:30), who knows our wants before we ask, “but to give man a sight of his misery, to humble his heart, to excite his desire.” Wesley puts it plainly: “Omniscient as he is, he cannot be informed of anything which he knew not before.” Calvin, for the Reformed, calls this “the single remedy sufficient for removing the superstition” of thinking advantage is gained by many words.

The witnesses meet the objection that, if God already knows, prayer is needless — the very Epicurean error Jerome named. They answer that prayer is not for God’s information but for our good: it humbles us, stirs our desire, exercises our faith, and prepares us to receive. The witnesses thus find in the verse both a rebuke and a comfort. The rebuke is to the heathen notion that God must be told and pressed; against it stands the truth that “your Father knoweth what things ye have need of, before ye ask.” The comfort is in the word “Father”: we come not to an aloof deity to be informed and persuaded, but to a Father whose knowledge of our needs goes before our prayers and whose love is readier to give than we to ask. The witnesses note that this truth, far from discouraging prayer, encourages it: we pray not to acquaint God with our wants but to acknowledge them, to seek from his hand what he already purposes to give, and to enjoy, in the asking, the fellowship of a child with a Father who knew the need before the cry. Such prayer needs no babble; a few words from a trusting heart suffice.

The witnesses add that the Father’s foreknowledge is the deepest encouragement to pray simply and trustfully. We need not labour to explain or persuade, for he knows our wants before we speak; we come not to inform but to commune, not to overcome reluctance but to receive from love. So the verse frees prayer from anxiety and artifice, and makes it the glad converse of a child with a Father whose readiness to give runs ahead of every request.
9:
“After this manner therefore pray ye: Our Father which art in heaven, Hallowed be thy name.” The witnesses receive the model prayer Christ gives in place of heathen babble. The grammatical-historical commentators note “after this manner” (houtōs, “thus”): Ellicott observes it “sanctions at once the use of the words themselves and of other prayers after the same pattern,” and Robertson would call it “the Model Prayer.” Barnes praises it as “unequalled for comprehensiveness and beauty.” Calvin, for the Reformed, notes from Luke’s setting that Christ does not bind his people to a fixed form of words, though the form is most fit to be used. The address “Our Father” the fathers cherish: Cyprian, that “the Son who taught us to pray” gives us boldness to call God Father; the Eastern Chrysostom, that in that one title we confess “the forgiveness of sins, the adoption, the inheritance, the brotherhood with the Only-begotten.”

The Latin Pseudo-Chrysostom notes the breadth of “Our” — we pray not for ourselves only but, “brotherly charity instigating,” for all — and that “which art in heaven” lifts the heart to “a heavenly Father.” On “Hallowed be thy name,” the first petition, the witnesses agree it asks not that God’s name be made holy (it is holy already) but that it be hallowed, reverenced, and glorified by us and all men. The witnesses thus find in the opening of the prayer the right order and spirit of all approach to God. Before any request for ourselves, we own God as Father — near, loving, and ours — yet “in heaven,” exalted and holy; and we put his glory first, praying that his name be hallowed. The witnesses note how this disposes the heart: the child comes confidently, yet reverently; seeks the Father’s honour before his own supply; and rises above the heathen’s self-serving petitions to the worship and trust that befit a son of God. The model prayer thus begins where prayer should begin — with the Father, and with his hallowed name.

The witnesses dwell on the perfect balance of the address. ‘Our Father’ gives confidence, for we come to one who loves us; ‘which art in heaven’ gives reverence, for we come to the exalted and holy God. Together they teach the spirit of all true prayer — bold yet humble, intimate yet awed. And the first petition, that his name be hallowed, sets God’s glory before our needs, so that the disciple learns to seek the Father’s honour first and his own supply after.
10:
“Thy kingdom come. Thy will be done in earth, as it is in heaven.” The witnesses unfold the second and third petitions, which seek God’s reign and obedience before any thought of self. The grammatical-historical commentators explain “thy kingdom come” as the prayer “that God may reign everywhere, that his laws may be obeyed” (Barnes), the kingdom being his reign (cf. Matthew 3:2); Ellicott traces it to the Messianic hope (Isaiah 11:1–6), and Clarke recalls the Jewish saying that “he prays not at all in whose prayers there is no mention of the kingdom of God.” On “thy will be done,” the Latin Cyprian explains, “we ask not that God may do his own will, but that we may be enabled to do what he wills,” and to do it “in us”; Pseudo-Chrysostom notes that “as in heaven, so in earth” may govern all three preceding petitions.

The fathers dwell on the heavenly pattern. The Eastern Chrysostom marvels that, before we come to heaven, Christ “bids us make this earth into heaven” by the doing of God’s will; the angels do it perfectly above, and we pray to do it so below. Jerome rebukes those who imagine “daily falls in heaven,” the heavenly will being done without fail. The witnesses thus find in these petitions the ordering of the disciple’s desires under God. Having hallowed the Father’s name, he prays for the coming of his kingdom — the spread of his reign in hearts and over the world, and at last its full appearing — and for the doing of his will on earth as the angels do it in heaven: gladly, fully, without delay. The witnesses note how these petitions enlarge the soul beyond its own concerns to the glory of God and the good of all: to pray “thy kingdom come, thy will be done” is to seek first the kingdom (v.33), to subordinate every private wish to God’s purpose, and to long that earth might mirror heaven. Only after these does the prayer descend to ask for bread, teaching that God’s glory and reign must come before our own supply.
11:
“Give us this day our daily bread.” The witnesses note that here the prayer turns from God’s glory to our need, and they debate the meaning of “daily.” The grammatical-historical commentators dwell on the rare word epiousion: Clarke says he finds “upwards of thirty different explanations,” the word being “found in no Greek author” before; Robertson and Ellicott confess the obscurity. The chief readings are three — “daily” (for the day), “for the morrow” (the next day’s bread, given today, Gill, Lightfoot), and “supersubstantial” (Jerome’s rendering, taken by some of a spiritual or eucharistic bread). Barnes takes “bread” for “everything necessary to sustain life” (cf. Matthew 4:4; Deuteronomy 8:3).

The fathers offer the spiritual senses. The Latin Cyprian and the Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem read the bread of Christ himself, “the bread of life,” the “substantial” bread of the Eucharist; Gregory notes we call it “our bread, yet pray it be given us, for it is God’s to give.” Most of the grammatical commentators, however, take it first of the body’s daily provision. The witnesses thus find in the petition both humility and trust. After seeking God’s name, kingdom, and will, the disciple is taught to ask for bread — the simplest necessity — and that only “this day,” one day’s supply at a time. The witnesses note the lessons folded into so small a request: that we depend on God for the commonest things; that we may ask him for our bodily needs, not only our spiritual; that we are to seek a sufficiency, not abundance, and for the day, not for hoarded years (forbidding anxious care, v.34); and that even our bread is a gift, not a wage. Whether the bread be of the body or, with the fathers, also of the soul fed by Christ, the petition teaches the disciple to live in daily dependence on the Father who feeds him.

The witnesses note how much humility lies in so small a request. The disciple who has prayed for God’s kingdom and will now asks only for bread, and only for the day — owning his dependence for the commonest things, and content with a sufficiency. So the verse forbids both pride and anxious care: pride, by teaching that even our bread is a gift; and care, by teaching us to ask only for today, trusting the Father for tomorrow.
12:
“And forgive us our debts, as we forgive our debtors.” The witnesses note that, after bread for the body, the prayer asks pardon for the soul. The grammatical-historical commentators explain “debts” as a figure for sins: Gill notes that “nothing is more frequent in the Jewish writings than to call sins debts,” and Robertson and Ellicott observe that a duty unfulfilled is a debt unpaid (Luke has “sins,” Luke 11:4). Sin is a debt we owe to God’s justice and cannot pay, for which we must seek forgiveness. The clause “as we forgive our debtors” the commentators take not as the measure or merit of God’s pardon, but as its necessary accompaniment and evidence — the forgiven heart forgives.

The fathers press both the universality of the need and the condition. The Latin Augustine wields the petition against the Pelagians who claimed a sinless perfection in this life: even the righteous must daily pray “forgive us our debts.” The Eastern Chrysostom notes that the prayer, with its address “Father,” is for the faithful, who yet need forgiveness. Cyprian marks the order — after food, pardon, “that he who is fed of God may live in God.” On the condition, Gregory says “that good which in our penitence we ask of God, we should first bestow on our neighbour,” and Pseudo-Chrysostom warns the unforgiving: with what hope does he pray who “cherishes hatred,” praying “with a falsehood on his lips” when he says “I forgive”? The witnesses thus find in the petition a humbling and a searching truth. Humbling, that even the holiest must daily confess himself a debtor and seek pardon, having no plea but mercy. Searching, that the prayer for forgiveness is bound to the forgiving of others: not that our forgiveness earns God’s, but that an unforgiving spirit proves the heart unforgiven, and the lips that ask mercy while withholding it lie before God. The disciple who would be forgiven must himself forgive.

The witnesses press the searching link between being forgiven and forgiving. The petition will not let the disciple seek mercy while harbouring resentment, for the unforgiving heart shows itself unforgiven. So the verse joins the vertical and the horizontal: as we receive pardon from God, we must extend it to men, and the daily prayer for forgiveness becomes a daily summons to forgive. None may ask of God what he refuses to his brother.
13:
“And lead us not into temptation, but deliver us from evil: For thine is the kingdom, and the power, and the glory, for ever. Amen.” The witnesses unfold the last petition and the doxology. The grammatical-historical commentators face the difficulty that God “tempts no man” (James 1:13): they take “lead us not into temptation” to mean, not that God entices to sin, but that he would not suffer us to be led into trial beyond our strength, nor abandon us to the power of temptation — a prayer of distrust in self and dependence on God’s keeping. “Deliver us from evil” they read either of evil in general or, with many, of “the evil one,” Satan (as the Greek allows). On the closing doxology, the commentators note that it is absent from the oldest manuscripts and so is judged by most to be no original part of Matthew’s text, though ancient and fitting — a liturgical ascription gathered from 1 Chronicles 29:11.

The fathers and Reformers draw out the spirit of the petition and the praise. To pray “lead us not into temptation” is to own our weakness and to cast ourselves on God, who alone can keep us from falling; to ask deliverance “from the evil one” is to seek rescue from the great enemy of souls. And the doxology, whether original or added in worship, fitly crowns the prayer: having asked all our needs of the Father, we end by ascribing to him “the kingdom, and the power, and the glory, for ever” — owning that the kingdom we prayed might come is his, the power to grant our requests is his, and the glory of all is his. The witnesses thus find the model prayer ending as it began, with God. It opened with the Father and his hallowed name; it closes with his kingdom, power, and glory; and between, it gathers every need of body and soul into petitions ordered under his honour. The “Amen” seals it as the prayer of faith. The witnesses note how the whole teaches the disciple to pray — reverently, trustfully, briefly, and with God first and last — the perfect pattern against the heathen’s empty babble.
14:
“For if ye forgive men their trespasses, your heavenly Father will also forgive you.” The witnesses note that Christ singles out, of all the petitions, the one on forgiveness for special enforcement. The grammatical-historical commentators observe that this expands the clause of the prayer (v.12): Calvin explains that Christ here gives “the reason why that condition was added” — “God will not be ready to hear us, unless we also show ourselves ready to grant forgiveness.” Clarke draws the proportion: “for a king to forgive his subjects a hundred millions” of offences, while we will not forgive a brother a trifle, is monstrous ingratitude (cf. Matthew 18:23–35). The Latin Augustine notes that the Lord “judged that petition most worthy of further enforcement which relates to forgiveness.”

The fathers press the likeness to God that forgiving brings. The Eastern Chrysostom: Christ “mentions heaven and the Father to claim our attention, for nothing so likens you to God as to forgive him who has injured you.” Cyprian warns that “no excuse will abide you in the day of judgment, when you will be judged by your own sentence,” for “as you have dealt toward others, will be dealt with yourself.” Pseudo-Chrysostom notes the order: God does not forgive us first, but bids us forgive, knowing the treacherous heart. The witnesses thus find in the verse the great condition and motive of mercy. The condition is plain: he who would be forgiven must forgive; the forgiving of others is not the price of God’s pardon but the necessary mark of a heart that has received it, and its absence is the proof that pardon has not been received. The motive is the imitation of God: to forgive is to be like the Father, who forgives freely, and to show oneself his child. The witnesses note the solemn corollary, drawn out in the next verse — that the unforgiving shut themselves out from the forgiveness they ask, judged by their own refusal.

The witnesses observe that this is the only petition of the prayer Christ stops to explain, marking its weight. He will have his people know that the forgiveness they daily seek is bound to the forgiveness they daily owe. So the verse sets the disciple under a gracious necessity: having received mercy, he must show it; and the measure he metes will be measured to him again. To be a child of the forgiving Father is to bear his likeness in forgiving others.
15:
“But if ye forgive not men their trespasses, neither will your Father forgive your trespasses.” The witnesses note the solemn reverse of the promise. The grammatical-historical commentators draw the plain inference: where men are “not of a forgiving temper,” they cannot “expect” forgiveness from God (Gill); Clarke is severe — “a vindictive man excludes himself from all hope of eternal life” (cf. Ephesians 4:31; James 2:13), and “he who does not awake at the sound of so loud a voice is not asleep but dead.” The teaching is constant in Scripture and stated here without qualification.

The witnesses dwell on the searching weight of the warning. It does not mean that our forgiving earns God’s pardon, nor that the unforgiving could be otherwise saved but for this one fault; rather, an unforgiving spirit reveals a heart still unhumbled and unrenewed, which has never truly received the grace it claims. The man who, having been forgiven an unpayable debt by God, will not forgive his brother a trifle, shows that he has not understood or felt his own forgiveness; and such a one is justly left under his sins. The witnesses thus find in the verse a mirror for the professing Christian. To pray “forgive us as we forgive” while nursing resentment is to pronounce one’s own condemnation. The cherished grudge, the refusal to be reconciled, the holding of an injury — these are not small things but signs that the soul is yet a stranger to the pardoning grace of God. The witnesses note the mercy hidden even in the warning: it drives the unforgiving to examine and soften their hearts, that they may forgive and be forgiven; for the door of God’s mercy, open to all who come, is shut by our own hand when we shut it against our brother.

The witnesses press the verse as a mirror for self-examination. The cherished grudge is not a small fault but a sign that the heart is yet unrenewed; for one truly humbled by the greatness of his own forgiveness cannot withhold forgiveness from a brother. So the verse drives the unforgiving to look within and to soften, lest, while praying for pardon, they pronounce their own doom. The door of mercy, open to all who come, is shut only by the hand that shuts it against another.
16:
“Moreover when ye fast, be not, as the hypocrites, of a sad countenance: for they disfigure their faces, that they may appear unto men to fast. Verily I say unto you, They have their reward.” The witnesses note that the same vice condemned in alms and prayer is now exposed in fasting. The grammatical-historical commentators explain that Christ speaks chiefly of private fasts (Gill), and that the hypocrites “disfigure their faces” — Lightfoot, with ashes or a “sad countenance” (Robertson, skythrōpoi, “sullen,” putting on a gloomy look) — “that they may appear unto men to fast.” Wesley notes that Christ “does not enjoin fasting” here any more than alms or prayer, all being established duties, but corrects the manner. Ellicott observes that under the Pharisees fasting had risen “into a new prominence” beyond the one fast of the Law.

The fathers press the emptiness of an ostentatious fast. Gregory: “by the pale countenance, the trembling limbs… nothing is in the mind but the esteem of men.” Leo warns that a fasting which comes “not of reasons of continence but of the arts of deceit” is not pure. The Eastern Chrysostom laments that some have “even surpassed the hypocrites” in vanity. The witnesses thus find in the verse the third instance of the chapter’s great lesson: that religious acts done to be seen of men are paid in the coin of men’s praise and lose the reward of God. Fasting, like alms and prayer, is good; but the hypocrite turns even self-denial into self-display, advertising his abstinence by a studied gloom, that men may admire his devotion. The witnesses note the absurdity exposed: the fast meant to humble the soul before God is made a parade before men, and the very mortifying of the flesh becomes a feeding of pride. “They have their reward” — the notice they sought — and nothing beyond. The disciple is warned to fast, if he fasts, for God alone, and to keep the discipline hidden, lest the praise of men devour its fruit.

The witnesses note that fasting, rightly used, is a help to prayer and a discipline of the soul, but is wholly spoiled when turned to display. The studied gloom of the hypocrite makes self-denial a means of self-glory, the very opposite of its design. So the verse warns that no act of devotion is safe from the love of praise, and that even the mortifying of the flesh may feed the pride of the heart. The disciple is to fast unto God alone, and to hide it from men.
17:
“But thou, when thou fastest, anoint thine head, and wash thy face.” The witnesses note the positive counterpart — to keep the fast hidden under one’s ordinary appearance. The grammatical-historical commentators explain that, by Jewish custom, those who fasted “neither anointed themselves nor washed” (Lightfoot; cf. 2 Samuel 12:20), so to anoint and wash was to appear as on any other day — Barnes: “appear as you do daily; do not assume any new appearance.” Jerome notes Christ “speaks in accordance with the manner of Palestine,” where the head was anointed on festival days. The Latin Augustine cautions that this is not a literal command to anoint the head whenever we fast, but a figure for concealing the fast.

The fathers also read it morally. Gregory: “God approves that fasting which… opens the hands of alms,” bidding us bestow on another what we deny ourselves. Leo: fasting must be “fulfilled not in abstinence of food only, but much more in cutting off vices.” The Gloss reads the anointing of the head and washing of the face as the inward gladness and cleansing that should accompany true fasting. The witnesses thus find in the verse the manner of acceptable fasting. As alms are to be secret and prayer in the closet, so the fast is to be hidden, the disciple going about with his usual cheerful face, that none may guess his abstinence. The point, the witnesses agree, is not literal anointing but the avoidance of all display: the fast is between the soul and God, and is to be kept from the eyes of men. They note the deeper lesson the fathers add — that true fasting is more than abstaining from food; it is the denying of self, the cutting off of sin, and the turning of what is saved to the relief of the poor. The face washed and the head anointed picture a fasting that is glad, hidden, and unto God, not the sullen parade of the hypocrite.

The witnesses draw from the command a picture of the cheerful, hidden fast. The disciple is not to advertise his abstinence by a mournful face, but to go about as on any day, his fasting known to God alone. And the fathers add that true fasting reaches beyond the body to the soul — cutting off sin, opening the hand to the poor, and seeking God rather than the esteem of men. So the verse teaches that the worth of fasting lies not in its outward severity but in its inward sincerity and secrecy.
18:
“That thou appear not unto men to fast, but unto thy Father which is in secret: and thy Father, which seeth in secret, shall reward thee openly.” The witnesses note the close of the threefold teaching, repeating the refrain of secrecy and the seeing Father. The grammatical-historical commentators mark that this is “just the reverse of the hypocrites” (Gill) — the disciple fasts not to “appear unto men” but “unto thy Father which is in secret.” Robertson notes again that the word “openly” is no part of the genuine text (as in vv.4, 6); the reward is the Father’s, who “seeth in secret.” Clarke draws the application: “let us not be afraid that our hearts can be concealed from God,” but rather fear lest he find us “more desirous of the praise of men than of God.”

The witnesses dwell on the unity of the three lessons — alms, prayer, fasting — sealed by the same words. In each, the same temptation is met by the same remedy: do the duty for God, not for men; keep it secret; trust the Father who sees in secret to reward. The witnesses thus find in the verse the summary of the chapter’s first teaching. Whether giving, praying, or fasting, the disciple is to have one audience and one aim — the Father — and to flee the poison of doing religion to be seen. The witnesses note the gracious assurance that crowns each: “thy Father which seeth in secret.” The hypocrite needs an audience because he has no other reward; the disciple needs none, for the Father sees, and his seeing is enough. To fast unto God, with a washed face and a hidden heart, content that the Father beholds, is to lay up a reward that the praise of men can neither give nor take away. The witnesses thus read the verse as fixing, for all the acts of piety, the single principle of the secret life with God — the eye on the Father, and the reward from him.

The witnesses observe how the same refrain closes all three lessons, binding alms, prayer, and fasting under one rule. In each, the temptation is to be seen of men, and in each the remedy is the same — to act for the Father who sees in secret. So the verse fixes the principle of the whole hidden life with God: one audience, one aim, one reward. The disciple who learns it is freed at once from the craving for human praise and assured of the notice and recompense of his Father.
19:
“Lay not up for yourselves treasures upon earth, where moth and rust doth corrupt, and where thieves break through and steal.” The witnesses note the turn from ostentation to covetousness, the two great snares of the heart. The grammatical-historical commentators explain that ancient “treasures” lay in clothes and changes of raiment as well as gold and gems (Barnes, Rabanus), so the three perils named — “moth” (devouring garments), “rust” or corrosion (eating metals), and “thieves” — cover every kind of earthly wealth. Robertson reads the present imperative, “do not have this habit” of hoarding (mē thēsaurizete); Ellicott renders it pointedly, “treasure not up your treasures.” Clarke marks the folly: “what blindness for a man to lay up as a treasure that which must necessarily perish!”

The fathers expose the spiritual peril. The Eastern Chrysostom notes that, having driven out vanity, Christ fitly proceeds to “contempt of riches,” for “there is no greater cause of the desire of money than the love of praise.” The Latin Augustine warns that if one works “with the mind of gaining an earthly good,” his heart cannot “be pure while it is thus walking on earth.” Jerome extends it beyond money to “all our possessions,” for “every man serves that to which he is in bondage.” Henry, for the Puritan expositors, calls worldly-mindedness “as fatal a symptom of hypocrisy as any other.” The witnesses thus find in the verse a charge against the hoarding heart. Christ forbids not the having or the prudent use of earthly goods, but the laying-up of them as one’s treasure — the setting of the heart’s hope and happiness on what moth, rust, and thief can destroy. The witnesses note the wisdom of the warning: earthly riches are by their nature perishing and insecure, exposed to decay and loss, so that to treasure them is to build one’s joy on sand. The disciple is called to a higher investment, named in the next verse — to lay up treasure where no corruption reaches and no thief comes.

The witnesses note that the warning is aimed not at wealth itself but at the heart that treasures it. To hold and use earthly goods is no sin; to make them one’s treasure, the hope and joy of the soul, is to build on what must perish. So the verse exposes the folly of the hoarding heart, which lavishes its care on things that moth, rust, and thief can take away, and calls the disciple to a wiser reckoning of where true and lasting riches are found.
20:
“But lay up for yourselves treasures in heaven, where neither moth nor rust doth corrupt, and where thieves do not break through nor steal.” The witnesses set the heavenly treasure against the earthly. The grammatical-historical commentators explain that to lay up treasure in heaven is to “have provision made for your eternal felicity” (Barnes) rather than to “exhaust your strength and spend your days” providing only for this life. Ellicott takes the heavenly treasures, with Luke’s parallel (Luke 12:33), as “the good works, or rather the character formed by them, which follow us into the unseen world.” Robertson notes the “rust” (brōsis) as that which “eats” or corrodes — from which the heavenly store is forever free. Gill takes the treasure as “the riches of glory, the joys of another world.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, explains that men “lay up treasure in heaven” who, “instead of entangling themselves in the snares of this world, make it their care to meditate on the heavenly life.” The witnesses thus find in the verse the wise alternative to earthly hoarding. The disciple is not merely forbidden to treasure earthly goods, but directed to a better investment — to lay up treasure in heaven, where it is safe forever. The witnesses dwell on the security of this store: no moth devours, no rust corrodes, no thief breaks in; what is laid up with God can never be lost. They note the means of such laying-up — faith, good works, charity to the poor (cf. Luke 12:33; 1 Timothy 6:18–19), the whole life of grace — by which the believer sends his riches on before him. The witnesses thus read the verse as Christ’s call to a heavenly prudence: since earthly treasure perishes and heavenly endures, the truly wise man will set his heart and his pains on the imperishable, making it his business to be rich toward God. The choice is between a store that decays and one that abides; and the disciple is bidden to lay up where his treasure cannot fail.

The witnesses dwell on the security and the means of the heavenly treasure. What is laid up with God is beyond the reach of decay and theft; it can never be lost. And it is gathered by a life of faith and love — by good works, by charity to the poor, by all that is done for God. So the verse calls the disciple to a heavenly prudence, sending his riches before him into the world to come, and laying up where his treasure, unlike all earthly store, shall abide for ever.
21:
“For where your treasure is, there will your heart be also.” The witnesses take this as the principle that explains the whole. The grammatical-historical commentators read it as a self-evident law of the soul: a man’s heart — his love, his thoughts, his desires — follows his treasure, fixing itself on whatever he most values. So the question of where one lays up treasure is no small matter of money, but the question of where the heart shall dwell. If the treasure is on earth, the heart is earthbound; if in heaven, the heart is heavenly.

The witnesses dwell on the searching force of the maxim. It exposes the true reason Christ forbids earthly hoarding and commands heavenly: not chiefly for the safety of the store, but for the sake of the heart, which is drawn after whatever it treasures. The man who lays up earthly treasure binds his heart to a perishing world; his affections, his cares, his hopes are all below, and he cannot rise to God. But the man who lays up treasure in heaven has his heart there — set on things above, longing for the eternal, loosened from the world. The witnesses thus find in the verse the key to the Christian’s use of money and of all things. The danger of earthly treasure is not the gold itself but the captivity of the heart; and the blessing of heavenly treasure is that it lifts the heart to God. The witnesses note the test the verse provides: a man may know where his heart truly is by asking where his treasure is — what he most values, plans for, and delights in. Where the treasure is, there the heart will be; and the disciple is therefore to treasure heaven, that his heart may be heavenly, dwelling already where his Lord and his hope are.

The witnesses find in this brief maxim the heart of the whole passage. The danger of earthly treasure is the captivity of the affections; its remedy is to treasure heaven, that the heart may dwell there. So the verse gives the disciple a sure test of his soul: let him ask where his treasure is, and he will know where his heart is. The wise man therefore lays up in heaven, that his love, his thoughts, and his hope may rise to God, and that he may dwell already where his Saviour is.
22:
“The light of the body is the eye: if therefore thine eye be single, thy whole body shall be full of light.” The witnesses take this proverb as an illustration of the heart’s singleness toward God. The grammatical-historical commentators explain the figure: as the eye is the lamp that lights the whole body, so the inner faculty — the purpose or affection — lights the whole life; Ellicott calls it “the spiritual faculty whose proper work is to give light.” A “single” eye is one that is sound and clear, fixed on one object; so a single heart, set on God alone, fills the life with light. The Latin Augustine reads “the eye here” as “our purpose: if that be pure and right, all our works… are good”; Hilary calls it “the light of the heart,” which “if it continue single and brilliant” illumines the whole.

The Eastern Chrysostom notes that Christ, having spoken of bringing the understanding into captivity to covetousness, now “transfers it to a sensible illustration.” The witnesses thus find in the verse the principle of single-minded devotion. The eye fixed on one object, undistracted and undiseased, gives clear sight; so the heart fixed on God, undivided by the love of money, gives a life full of light — clear in judgment, pure in motive, sound in conduct. The witnesses note the connection with what precedes (the treasures) and follows (the two masters): a heart that treasures heaven and serves God is the single eye; a heart divided between God and mammon is the diseased eye that darkens all. To have the eye single is to have one aim, one love, one master; and such singleness floods the whole man with the light of God.

The witnesses press the practical force of the figure. As a man with a sound eye walks surely in the light, while one with a diseased eye stumbles in the dark, so the disciple whose heart is fixed on God sees clearly and walks rightly, while the divided heart gropes in confusion. The verse thus makes everything turn on the inner aim: let the eye be single, the purpose undivided and set on God, and the whole life is flooded with light. There is no part of conduct, the fathers add, that is not governed by the heart’s one intention.
23:
“But if thine eye be evil, thy whole body shall be full of darkness. If therefore the light that is in thee be darkness, how great is that darkness!” The witnesses take the “evil eye” as the corrupt or divided purpose, and especially covetousness. The grammatical-historical commentators note that an “evil eye” was a Hebrew idiom for an avaricious, grudging disposition: Gill — “if thou art of a sordid disposition, of an avaricious temper… the sin of covetousness prevailing.” Ellicott: if “the spiritual faculty whose work is to give light be itself diseased,” the whole life is in darkness. The closing words press the horror: if the very light within — the conscience, the governing purpose — be itself darkened, “how great is that darkness!” Calvin reads “light” of the reason left in man after the fall, which, if corrupted, leaves the soul in deeper night.

The fathers draw the moral. The Latin Augustine and Gregory warn that if our purpose, which should be pure, is overclouded with “evil purpose,” our very works of seeming good become darkness. The witnesses thus find in the verse the dreadful consequence of a divided or covetous heart. As the single eye fills the body with light, so the evil eye fills it with darkness; the man whose aim is set on earthly gain, whose inner principle is corrupt, walks in a darkness that pervades his whole life — his judgment perverted, his conscience clouded, his good deeds spoiled at the root. The witnesses dwell on the special terror of the last clause: it is one thing for a man to be in darkness, but another for the very faculty meant to give him light to be itself dark; when conscience is corrupted and the governing purpose itself is evil, there remains no inner light to guide, and the darkness is total. The disciple is thus warned that all turns on the eye, the heart’s aim: let that be single toward God, and all is light; let it be evil, set on mammon, and all is darkness — and a darkness the deeper because it masquerades as light.
24:
“No man can serve two masters: for either he will hate the one, and love the other; or else he will hold to the one, and despise the other. Ye cannot serve God and mammon.” The witnesses take this as the decisive choice between God and money. The grammatical-historical commentators explain “mammon” as a Syriac/Aramaic word for riches (Jerome): wealth personified as a rival master. The point is the impossibility of a divided allegiance — a slave belongs wholly to one lord, and cannot give two masters the undivided service each demands. The Eastern Chrysostom notes that Christ, having restrained “the tyranny of avarice by many motives,” now “adds yet more,” showing that the love of money is not merely dangerous but incompatible with the service of God.

The Latin Augustine presses the seriousness: “whoso serves mammon serves him who, set over these earthly things for his perversity, is called by the Lord the prince of this world” — to serve riches is, in effect, to serve the devil. Jerome bids “the covetous man who is called by the Christian name hear this, that he cannot serve both Christ and riches.” The witnesses thus find in the verse the great either/or of the spiritual life. God and money each claim the whole heart; one cannot be given to both. The man who tries to serve both will, in the testing, hate or despise the one and cleave to the other — and since money is a jealous and engrossing master, the attempt to serve God and mammon together ends in the service of mammon and the forsaking of God. The witnesses note that Christ does not say a man may not have money, but that he may not serve it — make it his master, his trust, his aim. The disciple must choose: God or mammon, for the heart that is divided is, in truth, given to the world; and only the single eye, the undivided service of God, can stand.

The witnesses press the verse as the death of all compromise between God and the world. Many would keep both, giving God their worship and money their trust; but Christ declares it impossible, for each master claims the whole. The verse thus forces a decision that no one can evade: which shall be lord of the heart? And it warns that the attempt to serve both ends always in the service of mammon, for the world is a jealous master that will not share the throne with God.
25:
“Therefore I say unto you, Take no thought for your life, what ye shall eat, or what ye shall drink; nor yet for your body, what ye shall put on. Is not the life more than meat, and the body than raiment?” The witnesses are careful about the meaning of “take no thought.” The grammatical-historical commentators explain that the word (merimnate, Robertson) forbids not foresight or honest labour, but “anxious care” — the corroding, distracting worry that distrusts God (Ellicott; Gill). The Gloss puts it: “Be not withdrawn by temporal cares from things eternal.” Christ argues from the greater to the less: God has given the greater gift, life and the body; will he not give the lesser, the food and raiment that sustain them? “Is not the life more than meat?”

The Latin Augustine connects it to what precedes: he who would serve God and not mammon must not let the cares of the body enslave him. The Eastern Chrysostom notes that Christ speaks of the soul’s needs “according to common usage,” though the spirit needs no food. The witnesses thus find in the verse the great cure for worldly anxiety. Having forbidden the service of mammon, Christ forbids the anxious care that is its root and fruit — the fretting over food, drink, and clothing that betrays a heart distrustful of God. The witnesses note the wisdom of his argument: the God who gave the life will surely sustain it, and he who formed the body will not leave it unclothed; to doubt the lesser gift, having received the greater, is unreasonable as well as faithless. The disciple is not forbidden to work or to provide, but forbidden to worry — to let the cares of this life choke the seed (cf. Matthew 13:22) and crowd out the things of God. Trust in the Father who gave life is the antidote to the anxiety that the love of mammon breeds.

The witnesses underline that the command is not against industry but against worry. Christ would have his people work and provide, yet without the corroding care that betrays distrust of God. The verse thus draws the line between prudent foresight, which is duty, and anxious care, which is sin; and it grounds the freedom from worry in the goodness of the Father, who gave the greater gifts of life and body and will not withhold the lesser. To trust him is to be delivered from the fretting that the love of the world breeds.
26:
“Behold the fowls of the air: for they sow not, neither do they reap, nor gather into barns; yet your heavenly Father feedeth them. Are ye not much better than they?” The witnesses take this as the argument from the lesser creatures to the greater children. The grammatical-historical commentators note that Christ points to the wild birds, which neither till nor store, yet are fed by God; how much more will the Father feed his children, who are “much better than they” (Gill). The Latin Augustine guards against abuse: “some argue that they ought not to labour, because the fowls neither sow nor reap” — but the birds’ being fed “without labour” is not a warrant for human idleness; the lesson is trust in providence, not sloth, for the Gloss adds that “our own care is not enough, but Divine” care sustains us.

The witnesses dwell on the comfort and the reproof of the figure. The comfort: if the heavenly Father feeds the sparrows, that have no foresight and lay up no store, he will surely feed his own children, whom he values infinitely more. The reproof: the disciple who frets and worries shows himself of less faith than befits a child so dear to God. The witnesses thus find in the verse a tender argument against anxiety. The birds preach providence: they do not worry, yet they are fed; and the God who feeds them is not their Father, as he is the disciple’s. The witnesses note the careful balance — Christ does not commend the birds’ lack of labour as a model for men, who are made to work, but their freedom from anxious care; the birds gather their food, but they do not fret over it, trusting the provision God has appointed. So the disciple is to labour in his calling, yet without the corroding worry that distrusts the Father, knowing that the same hand which feeds the fowls of the air will not fail the children of the kingdom.

The witnesses dwell on the homely eloquence of the birds. Day by day they are fed, without barn or store, by the open hand of God; and the disciple who watches them is meant to read in their carefree provision the care of his Father. The verse thus turns a common sight into a sermon against anxiety, and shames the worry of those who, being God’s children, doubt the providence that sustains the sparrows. If the birds are fed, the children need not fear.
27:
“Which of you by taking thought can add one cubit unto his stature?” The witnesses take this as the argument from the futility of anxiety. The grammatical-historical commentators note the double sense of the words: the phrase may mean adding “a cubit to his stature” (height) or “a span to his age” (length of life), Robertson and Ellicott observing that the Greek allows either, and the smallness of the addition suiting the sense of life’s span. Barnes draws the argument from “extreme weakness and helplessness”: “with all your care you cannot” add the least to your stature or your days. Clarke calls it “the unprofitableness” of anxious care. Calvin, for the Reformed, notes the folly of a mortal man fretting over what is wholly beyond his power.

The witnesses dwell on the searching simplicity of the question. Anxiety is not only faithless but useless: it cannot accomplish the very things it frets about. A man cannot, by all his worrying, add a single cubit to his height or a single hour to his life; these lie in the hand of God alone. The witnesses thus find in the verse a sharp exposure of the vanity of care. If we cannot by anxiety effect the smallest change in ourselves, how foolish to torment ourselves with worry over food and raiment, which are no more in our power. The witnesses note the implied lesson: that the same God who orders our growth and our days, beyond all our anxious striving, orders also our supply; and that to fret is to attempt by care what only God can give, while neglecting the trust that would receive it. The disciple is thus taught that anxiety adds nothing but trouble — it cannot lengthen life or better the body — and that the wiser course is to leave these things, as he must, in the hand of the Father, and to be free of a care that profits nothing.

The witnesses press the simple logic that exposes the vanity of care. Anxiety cannot accomplish even the least of the things it frets about; it adds no inch to the body and no hour to life. The verse thus strips worry of all pretence of usefulness, showing it to be not only faithless but powerless. Since these things lie wholly in God’s hand, the disciple is freed to leave them there, and to spend on trust the strength he would waste on a care that profits nothing.
28:
“And why take ye thought for raiment? Consider the lilies of the field, how they grow; they toil not, neither do they spin.” The witnesses note the second argument, drawn from the flowers, against anxiety over clothing. The grammatical-historical commentators pass from food to raiment, “which is not so necessary as food” (Chrysostom): Christ bids them “consider” — study, learn from — the wild lilies of the field, which “toil not, neither do they spin,” yet are clothed by God in beauty beyond man’s art. The Latin Augustine cautions against over-allegorising: “the things instanced are not to be allegorized so that we enquire what is denoted by the birds or the lilies”; they are plain examples of God’s providence and care for the lesser creatures.

The fathers marvel at the beauty God lavishes on the perishing flowers. Jerome: “what regal purple, what silk, what web of divers colours from the loom, may vie with flowers?” The Gloss notes God’s providence “in those things that are beyond human industry.” The witnesses thus find in the lilies a fresh argument against anxious care. The flowers do nothing to clothe themselves — they neither labour nor spin — yet God arrays them in a loveliness that the looms of men cannot match. The witnesses note the force of the figure for the question of raiment: if God so clothes the flowers, which spring up and perish in a day and toil not at all, how much more will he clothe his children, who are of far greater worth. The disciple is thus called to learn from the lilies the lesson the birds taught of food: that the Father who beautifies the grass will not leave his children unclothed, and that anxious care over raiment, like care over food, distrusts the God whose providence reaches even to the flowers of the field. The point, the witnesses repeat with the fathers, is not idleness but trust — to mark how God clothes what neither toils nor spins, and to rest the more in his care.

The witnesses dwell on the lesson of the lilies as on the lesson of the birds. The flowers neither toil nor spin, yet God arrays them in beauty; so the disciple is taught that the same providence which clothes the field will clothe his children. The verse thus answers the care for raiment as the birds answered the care for food, bidding the believer learn from the wild flowers to trust the God whose hand adorns even the grass, and to be free of the anxious thought that distrusts his care.
29:
“And yet I say unto you, That even Solomon in all his glory was not arrayed like one of these.” The witnesses note the heightening of the argument by comparison with Israel’s most splendid king. The grammatical-historical commentators mark the emphatic “I say unto you” (Ellicott — “a formula of emphasis… with special force here”): the wild flower outshines Solomon at the height of his magnificence. Robertson notes “was not arrayed” (middle voice, “did not clothe himself”) — Solomon’s glory was put on from without, but the lily’s is given by God from within. Calvin, for the Reformed, draws the lesson: “the kindness of God, gloriously displayed in herbs and flowers, exceeds all that men can accomplish by their wealth.”

The witnesses dwell on the force of the comparison. Solomon was the richest and most glorious of kings, his court a byword for splendour (cf. 1 Kings 10:4–7); yet all his robes of state could not match the beauty of a single wild flower that God clothes without human art. The witnesses thus find in the verse the crowning of the lily-argument. If God arrays the perishing flower more gloriously than Solomon in his royal robes, then his power and willingness to clothe far exceed anything the wealth or labour of man can achieve; and the disciple, who is worth more than many flowers, need not fret over raiment. The witnesses note the rebuke to human vanity as well: the pride of dress and display, which men labour and spend to attain, is outdone by the unsought beauty God gives the grass; so that the believer is taught both to trust God for clothing and to value little the finery the world admires, since the Maker of the lilies can adorn his children better than they can adorn themselves. The God who outdoes Solomon in clothing a flower will not fail to clothe his own.

The witnesses dwell on the surpassing beauty God gives the perishing flower. The greatest splendour of the greatest king could not match the array of a single lily, clothed without toil by the hand of God. The verse thus both magnifies the providence that so adorns the grass and rebukes the vanity that labours and spends for finery, teaching the disciple to trust the God who outdoes Solomon in clothing a flower, and to value little the show the world admires.
30:
“Wherefore, if God so clothe the grass of the field, which to day is, and to morrow is cast into the oven, shall he not much more clothe you, O ye of little faith?” The witnesses draw the conclusion of the argument and the gentle rebuke. The grammatical-historical commentators note the lesser-to-greater force: Barnes and Clarke explain that the “grass of the field” — including the wild flowers cut down and, when dried, used to heat the “oven” (the Jews often baking with dried grass and stubble) — is clothed by God though it lasts but a day; how much more will he clothe his children, who endure forever. Lightfoot notes that “O ye of little faith” (oligopistoi) is a phrase “very frequent in the Talmudists,” a gentle reproof.

Clarke observes that Christ here “confounds both the luxury of the rich in their superfluities, and the distrust of the poor.” The witnesses thus find in the verse the heart of the whole teaching on anxiety — the rebuke of little faith. The argument is complete: if God so clothes the perishing grass, destined for the fire, he will surely clothe his children, who are of infinite worth and eternal destiny; to doubt it is not humility but “little faith.” The witnesses note the tenderness of the rebuke: Christ does not say “O ye of no faith,” but “little faith,” chiding gently those who have faith for great things yet falter over daily bread and raiment. They dwell on the diagnosis the phrase supplies — that anxiety is at bottom a failure of faith, a distrust of the Father’s care; and on the cure it implies — that growing faith dispels worry, for the more we trust the God who clothes the grass, the less we fret over our own needs. The disciple is thus called from anxious care to a fuller faith in the Father who, having clothed the field, will not leave his children bare.

The witnesses dwell on the gentleness of the rebuke. Christ does not condemn his anxious people as faithless, but chides them as ‘of little faith,’ owning the faith they have while calling it to grow. The verse thus diagnoses worry as a weakness of trust and points to its cure — a fuller confidence in the Father who clothes the grass. The more the disciple believes in the providence that adorns the perishing field, the less he will fret over his own needs, for the God of the lilies is his Father.
31:
“Therefore take no thought, saying, What shall we eat? or, What shall we drink? or, Wherewithal shall we be clothed?” The witnesses note the repetition of the command, now gathering up the arguments. The grammatical-historical commentators observe that Christ repeats “take no thought” to drive the lesson home: Remigius notes “he repeated this that he might show how highly necessary this precept is, and inculcate it more strongly.” Rabanus marks the precise wording — Christ forbids not seeking food, drink, and raiment, but the anxious “saying, What shall we eat?” — the fretful, doubting care, not the prudent provision.

The witnesses dwell on the form of the anxious question. To go about “saying, What shall we eat? what shall we drink? wherewithal shall we be clothed?” is to be consumed by the very cares Christ has shown to be needless: for the Father feeds the birds, clothes the lilies, and knows the needs of his children. The witnesses thus find in the verse the summary prohibition of worldly anxiety, drawn from all that precedes. Having argued from the greater gift of life, from the birds, from the futility of care, from the lilies and Solomon and the grass, Christ now sums it: “therefore take no thought.” The repeated command, the witnesses note, is not a counsel of carelessness but of confidence — the disciple is to be free of the fretting, distrustful anxiety that marks the heart without God, and to leave his needs, with quiet trust, in the Father’s hand. They observe that such anxious questioning is the mark of the Gentile (to be named in the next verse), who, not knowing the Father, must fret; but the child of God, who knows that his Father knows, is called to a holy freedom from care, busy in his calling yet untroubled in his heart.

The witnesses note that the repeated command marks the importance and difficulty of the lesson. Worry is so natural to the heart that Christ must press the precept again, gathering all his arguments into one charge. The verse thus calls the disciple to a settled freedom from anxious care, not by carelessness but by confidence in the Father; and it sets the fretful questioning of the worldly against the quiet trust that befits a child of God, who knows that his needs are known and provided for.
32:
“(For after all these things do the Gentiles seek:) for your heavenly Father knoweth that ye have need of all these things.” The witnesses note the contrast between the heathen’s anxiety and the believer’s Father. The grammatical-historical commentators explain that anxious seeking after these things is the mark of “the Gentiles” — those “destitute of the true doctrines of religion” (Barnes), who, not knowing God’s fatherly care, must fret. Ellicott marks the tone “of pity rather than of censure.” The remedy is the Father’s knowledge: “your heavenly Father knoweth that ye have need of all these things” — the Eastern Chrysostom noting that Christ says “not ‘God knoweth,’ but ‘your Father knoweth,’ to lead them to higher hope; for if he be their Father, he will not endure” to neglect them.

The Latin Augustine adds that God “foreknew, before all time,” what his people would need. The witnesses thus find in the verse both a reproof and a comfort. The reproof: anxious care is heathenish, the way of those who have no Father in heaven and must therefore worry; for a child of God to fret over food and raiment is to sink to the level of the Gentile who knows no providence. The comfort: the disciple has a heavenly Father who knows his needs — knows them perfectly, before they are felt or spoken — and who, being a Father, will surely supply them. The witnesses note the force of the contrast: the Gentile seeks anxiously because he knows no one who cares; the believer may seek calmly, or rather not be anxious at all, because he knows One who cares as a Father and provides as a Father. To worry, then, is to forget our adoption and to act as if we were fatherless; and the cure for all such care is the remembrance that our heavenly Father knows.

The witnesses press the contrast as both a rebuke and a comfort. To fret over food and raiment is to live like the heathen who have no Father in heaven; but the disciple has a Father who knows his every need before it is felt. The verse thus shames anxiety as a forgetting of adoption, and cures it by the remembrance of the Father’s knowledge and care. The child of God is freed from the worry that consumes the Gentile, because he is not, like the Gentile, fatherless.
33:
“But seek ye first the kingdom of God, and his righteousness; and all these things shall be added unto you.” The witnesses take this as the positive command that crowns the teaching on anxiety. The grammatical-historical commentators explain “the kingdom of God” as his reign and the gospel of it, and “his righteousness” as the holiness of heart and life he requires (Clarke) — the things of the soul and of eternity, to be sought “first,” before and above all earthly concerns. Robertson notes that here Jesus, “in answer to those who see in the Sermon on the Mount only ethical comments,” sets the kingdom at the centre. The promise follows: “all these things” — the food, drink, and raiment over which men fret — “shall be added,” given over and above, to those who seek the kingdom first (cf. 1 Timothy 4:8; Psalm 37:25).

Calvin, for the Reformed, marks the rebuke in the command: anxious care “argues a gross and indolent neglect of the soul,” men toiling for the body while starving the spirit. The witnesses thus find in the verse the great ordering of the disciple’s desires and the antidote to all worldly care. The remedy for anxiety is not merely to stop worrying but to seek first the kingdom — to set the heart on God, his reign, and his righteousness, so that the lesser things fall into their place. The witnesses dwell on the precious promise attached: those who seek the kingdom first need not seek earthly things anxiously at all, for they “shall be added” by the hand of the Father, thrown in as a free addition to the one great pursuit. They note the wisdom and the comfort of it: to make God’s kingdom the first business of life is at once the highest duty and the surest provision, for the Father who is sought in the kingdom will not let his seekers want for bread. The disciple is thus freed from care by a holy ambition — to seek first the kingdom and righteousness of God, and to trust that all else shall be added.
34:
“Take therefore no thought for the morrow: for the morrow shall take thought for the things of itself. Sufficient unto the day is the evil thereof.” The witnesses take this as the closing word against anxiety, now forbidding worry over the future. The grammatical-historical commentators explain that Christ, having forbidden anxiety for present needs, now forbids the borrowing of tomorrow’s troubles: Jerome notes that “tomorrow in Scripture signifies time future,” and the Gloss that Christ “now forbids anxiety for future things, such fruitless care as proceeds from the fault of distrust.” “The morrow shall take thought for the things of itself” — each day will bring its own concerns, to be met when they come; and “sufficient unto the day is the evil thereof” — each day has enough trouble of its own without adding the imagined burdens of the future.

The Eastern Chrysostom observes that “nothing brings so much pain to the spirit as anxiety and care,” and that to be anxious for the morrow is to multiply needlessly the day’s burden. The Latin Augustine and Hilary read “tomorrow” of the future generally, against which Christ guards the disciple. The witnesses thus find in the verse the final counsel of trust. The disciple is to live one day at a time, meeting the duties and trials of each day in their season, and leaving the unknown future in the hand of God. The witnesses note the practical wisdom of the saying: the troubles we borrow from tomorrow are mostly imaginary, and even the real ones are best met when they arrive, with the grace then given; to drag tomorrow’s cares into today is to crush ourselves under a weight God never meant us to bear at once. They dwell on the gentle realism of “sufficient unto the day is the evil thereof” — Christ does not promise a life free of trouble, but bids us take it day by day, trusting the Father for each day as it comes. The whole teaching on anxiety thus closes where it must: in the simple, daily trust of a child who seeks first the kingdom and leaves the rest, today and tomorrow, to his Father.
